derivations.title: Nuclear Density is Independent of Mass Number
derivations.priority_reason: Conceptual derivation. Often asked to prove that all nuclei have constant density.

given_assumptions: Nucleus is a sphere of radius $R$.
Radius $R = R_0 A^{1/3}$ (where $A$ is Mass Number).
Average mass of a nucleon is $m$.
derivation.required: Show that nuclear density $\rho$ is constant.

final_result.text: Since $m$ and $R_0$ are constants, density is constant (approx $2.3 \times 10^{17} \text{ kg/m}^3$).

examiner_notes: Students often forget to substitute R = R0 A^(1/3).
Must state that since A cancels, density is independent of the size of the nucleus.
question_forms: Show that the density of nuclear matter is independent of the mass number.
Prove that all nuclei have nearly the same density.

derivations.title: Law of Radioactive Decay (Exponential Law)
derivations.priority_reason: The core mathematical derivation of the chapter. High probability for 3 marks.
derivations.diagram_description: Exponential decay graph. N (y-axis) vs Time t (x-axis). Curve starts at N0 and drops asymptotically to 0. Points corresponding to Half-life (N0/2) and Mean-life (N0/e) are often marked.

given_assumptions: Number of undecayed nuclei at time $t$ is $N$.
Rate of decay is proportional to $N$.
examiner_notes: Negative sign in dN/dt = -lambda N is mandatory (indicates decrease).
Integration limits must be shown explicitly.
Graph is often required for the 3rd mark.
question_forms: Derive the expression N = N0 e^(-lambda t) for radioactive decay.
State the law of radioactive decay and deduce the relation for the number of undecayed nuclei at any time t.

derivations.title: Relation between Half-Life and Decay Constant
derivations.priority_reason: Standard follow-up to the decay law. Essential for numericals.

given_assumptions: Radioactive decay follows $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.
At half-life $t = T_{1/2}$, number of nuclei $N = N_0 / 2$.

steps.text: Take natural log (ln) on both sides:

examiner_notes: Do not confuse Mean life (1/lambda) with Half life (0.693/lambda).
question_forms: Derive the relation between half-life and decay constant of a radioactive substance.
Show that half-life is inversely proportional to the decay constant.

derivations.title: Average (Mean) Life of a Radioactive Substance
derivations.priority_reason: Involves integration by parts. Often distinguishes between average and top scorers.

given_assumptions: Decay rate $|dN/dt| = \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ (Number of nuclei decaying between $t$ and $t+dt$).
derivation.required: Mean life $\tau$ (Sum of all lifetimes / Total nuclei).

steps.text: Total lifetime of all nuclei = $\int_{0}^{\infty} t |dN|$.
steps.latex: \text{Total Time} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t (\lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t}) dt

steps.text: Integrate by parts: $\int u dv = uv - \int v du$. Let $u=t, dv=e^{-\lambda t}dt$.
First term is 0 at both limits. Evaluate second term:
steps.latex: \tau = \lambda \left( 0 + \frac{1}{\lambda} \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} dt \right) = \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} dt

examiner_notes: Integration by parts is the critical step.
Limits 0 to infinity must be used.
Relation tau = 1.44 T_half is often asked as a sub-question.
question_forms: Define mean life of a radioactive sample and derive its expression in terms of decay constant.
Show that the mean life is reciprocal of the decay constant.
